Month 2
CBT application submitted, space terms under discussion
Month 3
GO/NO-GO: CBT funding decision expected. Lease conditional on approval.
2

Pre-Launch

Months 4-6 — Fit-out, founding member campaign, systems setup
Gate: 15 founding members signed, space 80% ready
Space Fit-Out and Construction
Hire local contractor. Priorities: open plan with 12+ desks, 1-2 phone booths, meeting room (6-8 person), kitchenette, fast reliable power. Budget $20-45K. Use local trades to build community goodwill.
Operations
Furniture and Technology Procurement
Source ergonomic chairs ($150-250 each), height-adjust desks for dedicated spaces, meeting table, monitor arms. Tech: Gigabit fiber install, Ubiquiti or similar mesh wifi, Kisi or Brivo access control, 85" display for events. Budget: $12-22K furniture, $5-9K tech.
Capital
Founding Member Campaign
Email list launch: "Charter Members get 40% off for 12 months, locked rate forever." Target 15 sign-ups at $75/mo (Hot Desk rate). Revenue: $13,500 in committed Year 1 cash. Host one in-person info session at City Hall or Rossland public library.
Revenue
Management Software Setup
Implement Nexudus, Cobot, or OfficeRnD for member management, billing, room booking, and access logs. These platforms run $80-200/mo and pay for themselves by eliminating manual admin. Set up Stripe for automated billing.
Systems
Founder Prepares to Operate as Community Manager
The founder runs the space from day one at 80 hrs/month ($22/hr = $1,760/mo), scaling hours as membership grows. This means completing onboarding on management software, access control, and billing systems before soft launch. No external hire needed initially — the founder's direct investment in daily operations is the space's strongest asset in the early months.
Operations
Red Mountain and Trail Business Outreach
Reach out to Red Mountain Resort operations, Teck Coal Trail offices, and RDKB. Offer corporate day pass bundles. A single corporate account (10 day passes/month) generates $220/mo. Aim for 3 accounts before launch.
B2B
Month 4
CBT space terms agreed, contractor on site, founding member campaign live
Month 5
10 founding members signed, internet and access control installed
Month 6
GO/NO-GO: 15 founding members, CM hired, soft-open date set
3

Launch Week

Month 7 — Open doors and establish the brand
Gate: 20+ members on Day 30, 3 events in first month
Grand Opening Event
Public open house: Saturday 10am-4pm. Invite City of Rossland mayor, CBT Basin Advisor, local press, all email list subscribers. Free coffee, demo day passes, founding member sign-up table. Aim for 60-100 attendees. Budget $1,000 for catering and signage.
Launch
Free Day Pass Week
Offer 5 free day passes each to the first 20 people who sign up post-launch. This fills the space during the critical first week, creates social proof photos, and converts trial users to members. Track conversion rate closely.
Acquisition
Local PR Push
Submit press release to Trail Daily Times, Rossland Telegraph, and CBC Kootenay. Pitch a feature story around remote work and small-town economic resilience. One well-placed article generates more awareness than months of social media posting.
PR
First Month Event Calendar
Book 3 events immediately: (1) CBT Basin Advisor drop-in session, (2) Remote work skill share (productivity tools), (3) Kootenay entrepreneurship meetup. Events drive foot traffic and position the space as a hub, not just an office.
Community
4

Stabilize and Grow

Months 8-18 — Build toward sustainable operations
Gate: Month 12 operating surplus, CBT progress report submitted
Monthly KPI Review
Track: total members by tier, occupancy rate by time of day, meeting room booking rate, net promoter score (quarterly survey), revenue vs budget. Review at start of each month. Act on any metric 20%+ off target within 2 weeks.
Operations
CBT 6-Month Progress Report
Submit required CBT reporting at Month 13 (6 months post-opening). Include: membership numbers, revenue vs projection, community events hosted, employment supported. Strong reporting maintains CBT relationship and positions for future programs.
CBT Priority
Member Retention Program
At Month 6, survey all members. Identify churn risk. Introduce member-gets-member referral: one free month for each referral that converts. The cheapest acquisition channel you have is your existing members.
Retention
Manager Hours Review (Month 9)
If member count exceeds 25 and meeting room bookings are consistent, increase Community Manager to 25-30 hrs/week. Delay this if cash flow is below realistic scenario. The CM is the single biggest operational cost lever.
HR
Year 2 Expansion Assessment
At Month 15, evaluate: Are we at 80%+ occupancy? Is there a waitlist for dedicated desks or offices? If yes, begin planning for adjacent space or second location. If no, focus on non-member meeting room and event revenue to grow top line without adding fixed costs.
Strategy
CBT Annual Report (Month 19)
Full-year CBT report due at end of Year 1 of operations. This is the basis for CBT continued support and potential loan deferral. Include case studies of 3-5 members whose work was enabled by the space. Stories matter as much as numbers.
CBT Priority

CBT Partnership Framework

Columbia Basin Trust has two primary funding vehicles relevant to this project. Approach them as a long-term partner, not a one-time grant source. CBT advisors can make introductions to other regional partners and amplify your network significantly.

Impact Investment Fund
$50K - $500K
Low-interest repayable loans for projects delivering community, environmental, or economic benefit to Basin residents. Coworking has a direct economic development story. Application requires business plan, financials, and demonstration of community support. Turnaround: 60-90 days.
Working Capital Loan
Up to $40K
Short-term financing for eligible Basin businesses covering operating expenses during growth phases. Can supplement the Impact Investment Fund application or serve as a standalone bridge. Faster approval than the main fund.
Basin Business Advisors
No cost
CBT offers free business advisory services to Basin entrepreneurs. Use this before applying for funding — advisors help you strengthen your application and avoid common pitfalls. They also know which local programs can stack with CBT funding.
Hosted CBT Events
Partnership value
Offer Prism Works as a venue for quarterly CBT Basin Advisor drop-in sessions. This creates a direct link between CBT and your space in community members' minds, and is a concrete deliverable you can name in your funding application as community benefit.
Risk Register
Risk Level Mitigation
CBT funding delayed or declined
Application takes 90+ days or does not meet criteria		 High Parallel application to Columbia Basin Credit Union for conventional small business loan. Founding member pre-sales provide partial bridge capital. Do not sign a lease until either CBT approval or alternate financing is confirmed.
Demand below conservative scenario
Fewer than 15 members in first 6 months		 High Aggressive non-member meeting room and event rental. Reduce CM hours to 15/week. Partner with Trail businesses for satellite desk packages. Month 9 go/no-go review triggers restructuring plan if occupancy under 50%.
CBT space terms unfavorable
CBT imposes conditions or rates that change the financial case		 Low CBT already owns the target property, which eliminates third-party landlord risk entirely. Negotiation is with a partner, not an external landlord. If CBT cannot offer favorable terms for the identified space, the fallback is above-retail spaces on Columbia Ave or a sub-lease from an existing business. Risk is substantially lower than if CBT did not own the asset.
Seasonal demand variation
Summer drop-off as ski season workers leave		 Medium Summer programming: weekly workshops and events to maintain foot traffic. Day pass pricing promotion in off-season. Market to Trail commuters and remote workers year-round — these are not ski-dependent. Virtual memberships provide year-round baseline.
Key person dependency
CM departure destabilizes operations		 Medium Document all processes in Notion or similar from Month 1. Cross-train a trusted member as backup manager. Consider a small equity or profit-share arrangement with the CM after 12 months of strong performance to improve retention.
Competition from home office or Trail
Workers prefer Trail's lower cost options		 Low Rossland's identity as a lifestyle destination is a differentiator — the commute from Trail to Rossland is 15 minutes. Position on community quality and mountain lifestyle, not just desk availability. No direct competitor exists in Rossland currently.
Pre-Opening Checklist
  • BC company or C3 registered with CRA business number
  • CBT funding application submitted with supporting documents
  • Space agreement confirmed with CBT (lease or occupancy terms, rate, access date)
  • General liability and commercial property insurance active
  • Gigabit internet ordered and installation date confirmed
  • Access control system (keycard/app) installed and tested
  • Member management software configured with pricing tiers
  • Stripe account active; automatic billing tested end-to-end
  • 15 founding member contracts signed and first payment collected
  • Community Manager hired, onboarded, and trained on all systems
  • Fire safety, occupancy permit, and health authority sign-offs complete
  • Grand opening event date confirmed and invitations sent
  • First 30 days of events booked and promoted
  • Monthly financial reporting template prepared for CBT
Prism Works — Launch Roadmap — Annex C
Prepared for CBT Partnership Review — 2026

Submit required CBT reporting at Month 13 (6 months post-opening). Include: membership numbers, revenue vs projection, community events hosted, employment supported. Strong reporting maintains CBT relationship and positions for future programs.
CBT Priority
Member Retention Program
At Month 6, survey all members. Identify churn risk. Introduce member-gets-member referral: one free month for each referral that converts. The cheapest acquisition channel you have is your existing members.
Retention
Manager Hours Review (Month 9)
If member count exceeds 25 and meeting room bookings are consistent, increase Community Manager to 25-30 hrs/week. Delay this if cash flow is below realistic scenario. The CM is the single biggest operational cost lever.
HR
Year 2 Expansion Assessment
At Month 15, evaluate: Are we at 80%+ occupancy? Is there a waitlist for dedicated desks or offices? If yes, begin planning for adjacent space or second location. If no, focus on non-member meeting room and event revenue to grow top line without adding fixed costs.
Strategy
CBT Annual Report (Month 19)
Full-year CBT report due at end of Year 1 of operations. This is the basis for CBT continued support and potential loan deferral. Include case studies of 3-5 members whose work was enabled by the space. Stories matter as much as numbers.
CBT Priority

CBT Partnership Framework

Columbia Basin Trust has two primary funding vehicles relevant to this project. Approach them as a long-term partner, not a one-time grant source. CBT advisors can make introductions to other regional partners and amplify your network significantly.

Impact Investment Fund
$50K - $500K
Low-interest repayable loans for projects delivering community, environmental, or economic benefit to Basin residents. Coworking has a direct economic development story. Application requires business plan, financials, and demonstration of community support. Turnaround: 60-90 days.
Working Capital Loan
Up to $40K
Short-term financing for eligible Basin businesses covering operating expenses during growth phases. Can supplement the Impact Investment Fund application or serve as a standalone bridge. Faster approval than the main fund.
Basin Business Advisors
No cost
CBT offers free business advisory services to Basin entrepreneurs. Use this before applying for funding — advisors help you strengthen your application and avoid common pitfalls. They also know which local programs can stack with CBT funding.
Hosted CBT Events
Partnership value
Offer Prism Works as a venue for quarterly CBT Basin Advisor drop-in sessions. This creates a direct link between CBT and your space in community members' minds, and is a concrete deliverable you can name in your funding application as community benefit.
Risk Register
Risk Level Mitigation
CBT funding delayed or declined
Application takes 90+ days or does not meet criteria		 High Parallel application to Columbia Basin Credit Union for conventional small business loan. Founding member pre-sales provide partial bridge capital. Do not sign a lease until either CBT approval or alternate financing is confirmed.
Demand below conservative scenario
Fewer than 15 members in first 6 months		 High Aggressive non-member meeting room and event rental. Reduce CM hours to 15/week. Partner with Trail businesses for satellite desk packages. Month 9 go/no-go review triggers restructuring plan if occupancy under 50%.
CBT space terms unfavorable
CBT imposes conditions or rates that change the financial case		 Low CBT already owns the target property, which eliminates third-party landlord risk entirely. Negotiation is with a partner, not an external landlord. If CBT cannot offer favorable terms for the identified space, the fallback is above-retail spaces on Columbia Ave or a sub-lease from an existing business. Risk is substantially lower than if CBT did not own the asset.
Seasonal demand variation
Summer drop-off as ski season workers leave		 Medium Summer programming: weekly workshops and events to maintain foot traffic. Day pass pricing promotion in off-season. Market to Trail commuters and remote workers year-round — these are not ski-dependent. Virtual memberships provide year-round baseline.
Key person dependency
CM departure destabilizes operations		 Medium Document all processes in Notion or similar from Month 1. Cross-train a trusted member as backup manager. Consider a small equity or profit-share arrangement with the CM after 12 months of strong performance to improve retention.
Competition from home office or Trail
Workers prefer Trail's lower cost options		 Low Rossland's identity as a lifestyle destination is a differentiator — the commute from Trail to Rossland is 15 minutes. Position on community quality and mountain lifestyle, not just desk availability. No direct competitor exists in Rossland currently.

Critical Issues — Unresolved After Two Validation Cycles
Critical

Wrong Community Futures Office — Two Instances Now Found

This issue was first flagged in Cycle 2 and has not been corrected. It now has a second instance that was not previously identified.

Instance 1 — financial-model.html, CBT Impact Investment Fund administrator note:

Applications go to Community Futures Boundary in Grand Forks, not CBT head office.

Applications go to Community Futures South Kootenay (formerly Greater Trail), 825 Spokane Street, Trail, BC. Phone: 250-364-2595. Not CBT head office.

Instance 2 — business-plan.html, Risk Register row "CBT funding delayed beyond 90 days":

Community Futures Boundary as backup instrument.

Community Futures South Kootenay as supplementary instrument.

"Community Futures Boundary" is a separate organization serving the Grand Forks / Boundary region, which is a different geographic area from Rossland entirely. The correct office serving Rossland, Trail, Warfield, and Fruitvale is Community Futures South Kootenay (CFSK), formerly named Community Futures Greater Trail, renamed in late 2023. CFSK is at 825 Spokane Street, Trail, BC.

A CBT application naming the wrong Community Futures office will be caught immediately by any reviewer with regional knowledge. It flags the application as having unverified local intelligence, which is a poor start for a project whose central argument is deep local grounding.

Critical

Break-Even Figures Inconsistent Across Financial Model and CBT Application Brief

This issue was first flagged in Cycle 2. The CBT application brief still carries stale break-even figures that contradict the financial model. The financial model's figures were updated; the CBT brief was not. A reviewer reading both documents will see the contradiction.

ScenarioFinancial Model (current)CBT Brief (current)Required Fix
Conservative Month 15-17 Month 22 Update CBT brief to "Month 18-20" and financial model to match (see note)
Realistic Month 2 Month 6-8 Update CBT brief to "Month 2"
Optimistic Month 1 Month 3-4 Update CBT brief to "Month 1"

For the conservative scenario: linear interpolation between Y1 average monthly revenue ($46,412 / 12 = $3,868) and Y2 average ($68,180 / 12 = $5,682) to reach the $4,910 cost threshold yields approximately Month 18-20. The financial model's "Month 15-17" overstates this slightly; the CBT brief's "Month 22" understates it significantly. Both should converge on "Month 18-20."

File: cbt-application-brief.html — the financial-grid section, three fin-card elements. Update Break-Even rows to: Conservative "Month 18-20 (monthly)", Realistic "Month 2 (monthly)", Optimistic "Month 1 (monthly)."

File: financial-model.html — conservative scenario KPI card and assumption note. Update "Month 15-17" to "Month 18-20" to align with the linear interpolation result and match the CBT brief after correction.

New Findings — Cycle 3
New

Launch Roadmap Pre-Opening Checklist Contradicts Founder-as-Manager Structure

File: launch-roadmap.html — Pre-Opening Checklist, item 10:

Community Manager hired, onboarded, and trained on all systems

Founder trained on coworking management software, access control, and billing systems — ready to operate from day one

Every other document in the package states explicitly that the founder is the community manager. The Phase 2 task card in this same document even says "Founder Prepares to Operate as Community Manager." But the pre-opening checklist — which is the most likely document section a CBT reviewer scans quickly — still says "Community Manager hired," which implies a third-party hire. This inconsistency inside a single document is the kind of detail that erodes confidence in a funding application.

New

Red Mountain Resort Partnership Referenced as Fact in Optimistic Scenario — Should Be Aspirational

File: financial-model.html — Optimistic scenario assumption note states: "Red Mountain Resort partnership funnels digital nomads." No partnership has been established or even proposed. This language presents an aspirational relationship as an operational assumption, which overstates the certainty of the optimistic case.

The launch roadmap more honestly describes this as a pre-launch outreach goal ("Red Mountain and Trail Business Outreach" in Phase 2). These two framings should be consistent.

Fix: Change "Red Mountain Resort partnership funnels digital nomads" to "Active outreach to Red Mountain Resort operations to establish corporate day pass arrangement — target 3 accounts pre-launch." This maintains the optimism while being honest that the relationship is prospective, not confirmed.

Moderate Issues — Carried Forward Unresolved from Cycle 2
Moderate

Mountain CoLab Trajectory Comparison Still Present in Financial Model

File: financial-model.html — Realistic scenario assumption note contains: "Comparable to Mountain CoLab (Revelstoke) ramp trajectory."

Mountain CoLab is volunteer-run with no paid management — its cost structure is incomparable to a paid-founder model. It also prices hot desk at $65/month vs. Prism Works' $125/month. Revelstoke's population (~8,500) is roughly double Rossland's. This comparison appears to validate the realistic scenario but does not actually do so. A CBT reviewer who checks Mountain CoLab's structure will see the comparison fail on two axes.

Fix: Replace with: "25-member Year 1 target is consistent with rural BC coworking spaces operating with paid management in comparable mountain communities. The Mountain Coworking Alliance (MCA) — a network connecting members across BC mountain towns — is a potential affiliate program that could accelerate member acquisition and provide regional credibility."

Moderate

Rossland Population Still Listed as 4,100

File: business-plan.html — Community Character section states: "In a town of 4,100 people, word of mouth is the primary growth engine." The 2021 Canadian Census recorded Rossland at 4,328. Current estimates are approximately 4,455.

The market analysis section does not explicitly state the population figure, so only this one instance needs correction. Understating Rossland's population by 6% makes the market look smaller than it is — exactly the wrong direction for a CBT application.

Fix: Change "In a town of 4,100 people" to "In a town of approximately 4,300 people."

Moderate

Founding Member $75 Pricing Defined Nowhere in the Membership Section

The funding tables in both financial-model.html and cbt-application-brief.html reference "15 x $75/mo x 12 months = $13,500" as a revenue line. The membership tier table in business-plan.html shows no $75 tier. A CBT reviewer reading the membership section sees tiers from $22 to $600 — then encounters a $75 figure in the funding table with no explanation.

File: business-plan.html — Add a note below the membership tier grid, or after the Meeting Room callout box:

Founding Membership — Pre-Launch Rate
$75/month, locked for 12 months. Offered to the first 15 members who commit before opening day.
This is a 40% discount on the Hot Desk rate in exchange for early community validation.
Founding members convert to the standard $125/month Hot Desk rate at renewal.
Moderate

Manager Hours in Assumption Notes Do Not Match Dollar Figures — Still Unresolved

This has now been flagged in two consecutive validation cycles without correction. The stated monthly hours in the assumption notes, when multiplied by $22/hour, do not produce the dollar amounts in the cost rows:

Scenario / YearStated HoursHours Implied by CostDiscrepancy

Realistic Y2 120 hrs/mo 105 hrs/mo Notes overstate by 15 hrs/mo
Optimistic Y2 160 hrs/mo (full-time) 141 hrs/mo Notes overstate by 19 hrs/mo

The dollar figures are internally consistent with each other and plausible as business costs. The problem is the stated hours in the notes. The simplest fix is to update the assumption notes to reflect the actual implied hours, not the rounded figures.

Minor Issues — Low Priority, Carried Forward
Minor

Lease Savings Arithmetic Off by $200/Year in Both Documents

Market rate is $1,800/month; CBT community rate is $600/month; monthly savings = $1,200; annual savings = $14,400. Both documents show $14,600 in savings, producing figures that are off by $200:

  • financial-model.html — CBT rate note says "an additional $14,600/year"; Conservative lease card shows "+$2,092" (correct: +$1,892); Realistic lease card shows "+$43,312" (correct: +$43,112)
  • cbt-application-brief.html — Conservative fin-card shows "+$2,092" (correct: +$1,892); Realistic note implies +$43,312 (correct: +$43,112)

These are illustrative cards, not primary P&L figures, so the financial impact is immaterial. However, a CBT reviewer who spot-checks the arithmetic will catch it.

Minor

Optimistic Y1 Operating Costs $62,220 vs $58,920 for Other Scenarios — No Explanation

File: financial-model.html — Both conservative Y1 and realistic Y1 use $58,920 (12 × $4,910). Optimistic Y1 shows $62,220, which is $3,300/year ($275/month) higher. The optimistic assumption note only says "Founder scaling to full-time (160 hrs/mo) by Y2," implying Y1 is still at 80 hrs/month = $58,920. No explanation is given for the extra $3,300.

If the $62,220 figure is intentional (e.g., higher marketing spend at launch given the stronger optimistic case), add an assumption note. If it is a data entry error, correct to $58,920 and update Y1 optimistic net to $78,136.

Minor

Optimistic Y2 Requires 3 Private Offices — Exceeds Stated Layout Capacity

File: financial-model.html (optimistic scenario) and business-plan.html (operations section). The optimistic scenario requires 3 private offices in Year 2. The operations section describes the private zone as approximately 400 sq ft accommodating "1-2 enclosed private offices." A third private office at typical density (120-150 sq ft each) pushes the private zone to 360-450 sq ft for offices alone, plus corridor access — requiring expansion into the flexible zone.

Either note in the optimistic assumption that Year 2's 3 private offices requires zone reconfiguration, or cap at 2 private offices and adjust the revenue line accordingly.
